title: Ruthea interrupta
intro: Ruthea interrupta, also known as Devil's Claw, is a perennial plant species native to southern Africa, characterized by its claw-like roots and tubular flowers. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including the reduction of pain and inflammation, as well as its ability to aid in digestion and alleviate symptoms of arthritis. The bioactive constituents of Ruthea interrupta include harpagoside, a glycoside responsible for its medicinal properties, as well as other iridoid glycosides and phenolic compounds. Traditionally, the plant is used to make teas, capsules, and tinctures, which are among the most common herbal preparations made with Ruthea interrupta.
taxonomy: Ruthea interrupta is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, commonly known as the bittersweet family. It belongs to the genus Ruthea, which is a small group of flowering plants. This plant is native to New Zealand and is typically found growing in the wild in shaded areas. Within its family, Ruthea interrupta is further categorized based on its botanical and morphological characteristics. Further research may be necessary to determine its exact classification and relationships within the plant kingdom.
